![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FNv3FJWlvlis2RebsfhdH%252FUntitled.webp%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D00c8088e-a205-46b6-970c-29858dfee6dc&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=6d1f3da1&sv=2)

* * *

#### 🧪 eJPTv2 – eLearnSecurity Junior Penetration Tester (INE)

The **eJPTv2** is an entry-level, hands-on penetration testing certification designed to validate foundational skills in network, host, and web application security. Offered by INE (formerly eLearnSecurity), this certification is well-suited for aspiring pentesters and those looking to build practical experience before tackling more advanced certs like OSCP.

* * *

#### 📌 Overview & Exam Structure

- The exam consists of **45 questions** that must be answered within **48 hours** (reduced from previous 72 hours).

- You perform a penetration test on multiple target machines hosted remotely.

- Unlike earlier versions, the exam no longer requires a formal report submission, focusing solely on answering questions based on your pentest activities.

- You have **two attempts** to pass, with no penalty or mention of which attempt you passed on.

* * *

#### 🎯 What to Expect & Key Insights

- **Focus on gaining access to all machines first** — this is the core of the exam.

- The exam is **different but generally easier than a typical CTF** — it’s more structured but less tricky.

- Most candidates don’t get immediate access at the start; expect the first hour to be slow.

- After initial enumeration, you’ll find multiple vulnerabilities to exploit, so persistence is key.

- Breaks are important — I took ~4 hours of breaks and still finished comfortably within 10 hours.

- Don’t rush to finish early; after answering questions, spend time exploring machines to solidify understanding.

* * *

#### 🛠 Preparation & Study Strategy

- I combined official INE lectures with external resources like:

  - TheCyberMentor’s courses on Udemy/TCM Security

  - TryHackMe’s Jr Penetration Tester learning path

- Joining study groups (e.g., Telegram channels) helped me stay motivated and get peer support.

- Focus on **understanding concepts and techniques rather than just tools**.

- Take thorough **notes and build a structured cheat sheet** — I used Notion for easy organizing and reviewing.

- Complete **all labs** in the course before the exam. For difficult labs, dedicate plenty of time (20+ hours if needed) before viewing solutions.

- Practice manual exploitation and enumeration rather than relying solely on automated scripts.

* * *

#### 💡 Exam Tips & Lessons Learned

- Create **separate desktops/workspaces** for each target machine to stay organized.

- Always **keep detailed notes and screenshots** of commands, outputs, and credentials.

- Use your notes actively during the exam — most answers come from techniques taught in the course.

- Avoid hunting for “exotic” exploits during the exam — focus on what you know and what’s taught.

- Double-check all **flags before submission** — I lost points due to a copy-paste error on a flag.

- Manage your time wisely — breaks are necessary for focus, but don’t lose momentum.

- Expect some trial and error; persistence and methodical enumeration pay off.

* * *

#### 📚 Domain Breakdown & Passing Criteria (subject to change)

The exam covers four main domains with minimum score requirements:

Domain

Passing Score\*

Assessment Methodologies

90%

Host & Network Pentesting

70%

Web Application Pentesting

60%

Host & Network Auditing

80%

**Overall**

**70%**

\*Note: These requirements may be updated, as some candidates (including me) passed despite missing one domain’s threshold.

* * *

#### 🔍 My Personal Challenges

- Web application hacking remains my weakest area — it’s complex and requires continuous learning.

- I plan to pursue **eWPTX** next to deepen my web pentesting skills.

- Balancing study with college and life requires discipline — study groups helped me stay accountable.

* * *

#### 🎯 Final Thoughts

The eJPTv2 is a practical and realistic entry point into professional penetration testing. It tests your ability to conduct thorough reconnaissance, exploit vulnerabilities, escalate privileges, and document findings under time pressure. It’s not a walk in the park, but with steady preparation, it’s achievable and rewarding.

* * *
